query: can myometrial electrical activity predict preterm labor

retrieved_docs.title: Abnormal Labor -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Determining the progression of labor is a critical component of intrapartum care. Maternal uterine activity is assessed by manual palpation, external tocodynamometry, or monitoring with an intrauterine pressure catheter. Using external tocodynamometry, target uterine activity is 3 to 5 contractions in a 10-minute window. Effective contractions last 30 to 40 seconds. Intrauterine pressure assessment using a catheter is commonly used to measure the strength or intensity of uterine contractions in patients with ruptured membranes. This technique involves inserting a catheter through the cervix and into the uterus, which allows for direct measurement of intrauterine pressure. This monitoring employs Montevideo units (MVUs) as measurements of contraction intensity. Montevideo units are calculated by adding the sum of the net contraction pressures over 10 minutes. Adequate uterine activity is targeted at 200-250 MVUs. [21] Although this method of assessing uterine contractions has limitations, a more useful and accurate system has not yet been devised. Electrohysterography is a recent technique using externally applied abdominal electrodes, which monitors and records electrical voltages generated by myometrial contractions. This method of assessing uterine contractility is not currently part of standard clinical practice. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy Contractions -- Mechanism -- Mechanical Stretch
retrieved_docs.content: Mechanical stretch refers to tension on myometrial cells as the uterus distends. The physical stretching of the uterus results in an influx of ions, namely sodium and calcium, that change the action potential across myometrial cells. [5] [6] This change facilitates the onset of uterine contractions. The number of connexins (gap junctions) between myometrial cells increases before labor, allowing coordinated muscle contraction. These cell-to-cell contacts are less abundant early in pregnancy, favoring uterine quiescence. [7] There has also been a suggestion that uterine overdistention causes an "inflammatory pulse" that further activates myometrial contractility. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy Contractions -- Related Testing -- External Tocometer
retrieved_docs.content: Two probes are placed on the woman’s abdomen, 1 over the uterine fundus and the other in proximity to the fetal heart. This approach is the standard method of monitoring uterine contractions in laboring women.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy Contractions -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy Contractions -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: External tocometers and intrauterine pressure catheters are the primary clinical methods of monitoring myometrial activity. Although both devices allow visualization of contractions relative to the fetal heart rate, only intrauterine pressure catheters enable precise measurement of the strength of uterine contractions. There should be 3 to 5 contractions in the 10-minute window, each lasting 30 to 40 seconds. The monitoring of uterine contractions should be continuous during labor.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm labor (PTL) is defined as cervical change that develops in response to regular uterine contractions occurring between 20 0/7 and 36 6/7 weeks of estimated gestational age (EGA). The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) also states that PTL can be diagnosed in patients with regular contractions and cervical dilation of at least 2 cm before 37 weeks EGA, while the international guidelines from the World Association of Perinatal Medicine and the Perinatal Medicine Foundation (WAPM-PMF) recommend using a cutoff of at least 3 cm to diagnose spontaneous preterm labor (sPTL). [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Evaluation -- Biomarkers for PTL
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal fibronectin:  Fetal fibronectin is an ECM glycoprotein present at the decidual-chorionic interface that gets released into cervicovaginal secretions when the ECM is degraded. ECM degradation can be triggered by numerous causes, including many that also trigger PTL. Notably, a digital cervical exam, blood, or recent intercourse can all lead to false-positive results (which is why this sample is best collected before any other pelvic assessments). [27] The primary utility of checking for fFN in cervicovaginal secretions is its negative predictive value; the absence of fFN is associated with a low risk of birth within 7 days. [28] The risk of sPTB is increased with a positive fFN, though the positive predictive value of this test is still relatively low, limiting its clinical utility in some patients. [17] [29] [30] Conversely, patients with a cervical length between 20 mm and 30 mm and a positive fFN should be considered at high risk of sPTB within the next 7 days and managed accordingly. Qualitative fFN tests are typically considered positive when the fFN concentration is 50 ng/mL or more in cervicovaginal fluid. When a quantitative test is used, higher fFN concentrations are associated with higher rates of sPTB within 14 days. For example, results from a prospective study found the positive predictive value of sPTB increased from 32% to 75% when the concentration of fFN used to define a positive result was increased from 50 ng/mL to 500 ng/mL. [31] Unfortunately, quantitative fFN testing is not always readily available, including in the United States.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: PTL is diagnosed clinically based on symptoms and physical findings. ACOG's Practice Bulletin on the management of PTL states that the diagnosis of PTL is "generally based on clinical criteria of regular uterine contractions accompanied by a change in cervical dilation, effacement, or both, or initial presentation with regular contractions and cervical dilation of at least 2 cm." [1] The threshold for defining "regular contractions" has varied in the literature, ranging from 4 to 12 contractions per hour. [2] ACOG does not include a threshold in their definition, though the WAPM-PMF joint guideline recommends requiring 6 or more contractions per 30 minutes. [1] [2] WAPM-PMF recommends observing patients for at least 2 hours to assess contraction frequency and cervical change.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Labor: Physiology, Evaluation, and Management -- Pathophysiology -- First stage of labor
retrieved_docs.content: The active phase comprises the more rapid rate of cervical dilation that occurs from 6 cm to full dilation. During this phase, cervical dilation typically follows a more predictable rate. Around 95% of parturients will dilate between 1 and 2 cm per hr in the active phase, with multiparous individuals dilating faster than nulliparous individuals. Amniotomy may be considered if a delay in the active phase is suspected, eg, less than 2 cm of dilation is achieved in 4 hr, according to U.K. guidelines. If no progress follows the amniotomy, oxytocin may be administered to increase the strength and frequency of contractions.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Labor: Physiology, Evaluation, and Management -- Pathophysiology -- First stage of labor
retrieved_docs.content: The latent phase refers to the period of slow cervical dilation, typically from 0 cm to 4 cm or 6 cm. This phase is generally much longer and less predictable than the active phase. According to Friedman's classic curve, the latent phase can last up to 20 hr in nulliparous women and up to 14 hr in multiparous women. Sedation may increase the duration of this phase.

retrieved_docs.title: Variable Decelerations -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Intermittent variable decelerations are usually benign and do not result in poor perinatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Spontaneous preterm labor (sPTL) is characterized by cervical changes triggered by regular uterine contractions occurring between 20 0/7 and 36 6/7 weeks of gestational age (EGA). Diagnostic criteria vary, with the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists defining sPTL as cervical dilation of at least 2 cm before 37 weeks EGA, while international guidelines suggest a cutoff of 3 cm. Patients presenting with symptoms such as contractions, pelvic or back pain, vaginal bleeding, or fluid leakage undergo evaluations involving history, pelvic examination, and potentially transvaginal ultrasound or fetal fibronectin testing. Management strategies depend on gestational age and may include antenatal corticosteroids, tocolysis, magnesium sulfate, and group B Streptococcus prophylaxis to improve neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypotonic Labor -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Normal uterine contractions are generated in a coordinated fashion by two uterine pacemakers located at the cornua portion of the uterine fundus (fundal dominance) and propagated in a synchronous order towards the lower uterine segment. The downward coordinated path of contraction explains the role of contraction in the downward migration of the fetus and cervical changes. In the normal active phase, the contraction frequency is usually 3 to 4 within 10 minutes, with a pressure of 30 to 50 mm Hg above the resting tone. The minimum characteristics required to describe a contraction as effective are: Minimum frequent contractions with intervals less than 5 minutes apart Intensity of contractions greater than 25 mm Hg

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Evaluation -- Biomarkers for PTL
retrieved_docs.content: Other biomarkers for PTL: Positive PAMG-1 and IGFBP-1 tests, typically used to diagnose a rupture of membranes, are also associated with an increased risk of sPTB in patients with intact membranes. A prospective study of over 400 patients compared the use of placental alpha microglobulin-1 to insulin-like growth factor binding protein 1 in patients with PTL, intact membranes, and cervical lengths between 15 mm and 30 mm to determine their accuracy in identifying patients at risk of sPTB within 7 days. [32] This study's results showed that both biomarkers had a negative predictive value of approximately 97%, while the positive predictive value of placental alpha microglobulin-1 was significantly better at 61% compared to the positive predictive value of only 28% for insulin-like growth factor binding protein 1.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pregnancy Contractions -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Since uterine contractions are always present in a thriving uterus, they are differentiated based on frequency, amplitude, duration, and direction of propagation. Although there have been recent advances in knowledge regarding uterine contractions, there remains a huge gap in understanding the related physiology at the cellular and molecular levels. Knowledge of the process of uterine contractions that eventually leads to the expulsion of the baby helps clinicians identify abnormalities that may lead to obstetrical complications, such as preterm labor and arrest of labor. This also aids the pharmacists in developing and improving the drugs used for labor augmentation, induction, and tocolysis (see Image. Medications Used to Manage Abnormal Labor). Uterine contractions also play an essential role in minimizing postpartum bleeding, which explains why many drugs used to treat this complication target the pathway involved in myometrial contractility.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Uterus -- Mechanism -- Labor
retrieved_docs.content: The release of prostaglandins and oxytocin stimulates a positive feedback loop, continuing to increase the contractile strength of the uterus. These uterine contractions dilate and efface (soften, thin, and shorten) the cervix. Once again, a positive feedback loop begins where the dilation and effacement of the cervix further stimulate uterine contractions, which become longer in duration and more frequent as labor progresses. A uterus contraction starts in the fundus and sweeps down to the rest of the uterus. Uterine muscles shorten in response to the contraction, then relax, leading to fetal descent. Uterine contractions are measured in mmHg. The myometrium continues to contract after birth, causing the placenta to shear from the uterine wall for delivery through the birth canal. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: Uterine function includes the conditions رحم ناقص التوتر and عطالة رحمية ثانوية, which represent forms of reduced uterine tone and secondary uterine inertia, respectively, highlighting specific aspects of impaired uterine contractility that can affect labor and postpartum outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Poor reproductive history and elective termination of pregnancy are clinically associated with preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This relationship indicates that women who have experienced a suboptimal reproductive history or who have undergone an elective termination of pregnancy may be at increased risk for developing preterm labor. Recognizing these associations can help clinicians identify patients who require closer monitoring and targeted interventions to prevent or manage preterm labor, thereby improving maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Medical databases are clinically associated with a spectrum of obstetric conditions, serving as valuable resources for understanding and managing these entities. They are linked to مقدمات تسمم الحمل (early signs of preeclampsia), تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia), and الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (premature placental separation), providing data that aid in early detection, risk stratification, and therapeutic decision‑making for these serious pregnancy complications. In addition, databases are associated with تقلص الحوض (pelvic contraction) and تقلص مدخل الحوض (contraction of the pelvic inlet), offering information on uterine and pelvic dynamics that can influence labor progression and delivery planning. Finally, they are connected to gestation twin (twin pregnancy), supplying comprehensive records that support the monitoring of multiple fetuses, assessment of maternal‑fetal health, and coordination of specialized care. By integrating data across these conditions, medical databases enhance clinicians’ ability to deliver informed, evidence‑based obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in the third trimester is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that this cervical change is recognized as a sign of potential onset of labor before term.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2855
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history (REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR) is clinically associated with preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان), and preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان) is clinically associated with pelvic inflammatory disease (مرض التهاب الحوض). This interrelationship indicates that women with an adverse reproductive background may have an elevated risk of experiencing premature labor, and that premature labor may be linked to underlying pelvic inflammatory disease, underscoring the importance of comprehensive pelvic health assessment in patients with a history of poor reproductive outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 968
retrieved_docs.content: CAESAREAN SECTION SCAR TYPE UNKNOWN is clinically associated with الانفصال المبكر للمشيمة (early placental separation), مخاض قبل الأوان (premature labor), and بضع الرحم (uterine atony), indicating that an unknown‑type cesarean scar may predispose to placental detachment, early onset of labor, and loss of uterine tone. Early or threatened labor has a relationship with مخاض قبل الأوان, and it is further classified as تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) and مخاض مهدد (threatened labor), reflecting its role as a clinical category that encompasses both general threatened labor and the specific risk of premature delivery. The broader category LABOR AND PARTURITION includes مخاض مهدد as a parent concept, situating threatened labor within the overall framework of childbirth processes. Additionally, REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR is clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان, underscoring that a history of poor reproductive outcomes increases the likelihood of premature labor. Together, these interrelated concepts highlight how scar type, placental integrity, uterine contractility, prior reproductive history, and the classification of early or threatened labor collectively influence the risk and management of adverse obstetric events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2432
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester, is mapped to short cervical length in pregnancy, indicating that the condition of cervical shortening at any stage of gestation is understood as a short cervical length—a key obstetric measurement used to evaluate the risk of preterm birth and guide patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2115
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with a spectrum of obstetric conditions, including الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى, تقلص الحوض, تقلص مخرج الحوض, تقلص مدخل الحوض, تمزق الأغشية الباكر, حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ, نزف ما بعد الولادة, gestation twin, and فشل تحريض المخاض. These entities collectively represent key maternal‑fetal complications that are frequently recorded and analyzed in clinical information systems. Inflammatory disorders such as الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى (chorioamnionitis) can precipitate uterine activity, leading to تقلص الحوض (pelvic contraction) and its subcomponents—تقلص مخرج الحوض (pelvic outlet contraction) and تقلص مدخل الحوض (pelvic inlet contraction)—which may affect labor progression. Premature rupture of membranes (تمزق الأغشية الباكر) increases the risk of infection and can further stimulate uterine contractions or necessitate early delivery. Anatomical variations like حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ (bicornuate or otherwise abnormal uterine morphology) and multiple gestations (gestation twin) heighten the likelihood of labor dystocia, postpartum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة), and failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض). By linking these conditions within databases, clinicians can better identify patterns, anticipate complications, and tailor management strategies to improve maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 763
retrieved_docs.content: and risk factors of pre‑eclampsia, placental disorders, amniotic‑fluid deficiencies, uterine contractile events, and labor‑induction failures with both uncomplicated (normal) and high‑risk obstetric scenarios, including singleton and multiple gestations. Recognizing these interrelationships supports more precise risk stratification, diagnosis, and management of pregnant patients across the full spectrum of obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2862
retrieved_docs.content: Early or threatened labor is classified as تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر, indicating a condition in which signs of labor appear prematurely and the situation is identified as threatened preterm labor, a designation that guides clinical monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2539
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, second trimester, and cervical shortening, third trimester, are both classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that shortening of the cervix at either stage of pregnancy signals a heightened risk of labor onset before term and warrants close monitoring and appropriate management to address threatened labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2917
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, second trimester, is classified as early or threatened labor, indicating that this cervical change occurring in the second trimester is considered a sign of early or threatened labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1447
retrieved_docs.content: Cerclage (procedure) and REPRODUCTIVE HISTORY POOR are both clinically associated with قصور عنق الرحم. قصور عنق الرحم, also known as cervical insufficiency, is a condition in which the cervix is unable to maintain its structural integrity during pregnancy, increasing the risk of premature dilation and preterm delivery. The cerclage procedure involves placing a suturing stitch around the cervix to reinforce it and prevent premature opening, thereby directly addressing the mechanical weakness characteristic of قصور عنق الرحم. Additionally, a poor reproductive history—such as prior miscarriages, preterm births, or other adverse pregnancy outcomes—has been linked to a higher likelihood of developing قصور عنق الرحم, indicating that past obstetric complications can serve as a clinical indicator for this cervical disorder. Recognizing both the therapeutic role of cerclage and the predictive value of reproductive history is essential for early identification and management of patients at risk for قصور عنق الرحم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2821
retrieved_docs.content: Threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر) is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that individuals who are shorter in height may have an increased risk of experiencing preterm labor. This association highlights the importance of recognizing short stature as a potential clinical indicator when assessing pregnant patients for the likelihood of premature delivery, thereby informing monitoring strategies and preventive care measures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2010
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized under other complications of birth and puerperium affecting management of the mother, indicating its relevance to maternal care during and after delivery. This condition encompasses two specific forms: cervical shortening in the second trimester and cervical shortening in the third trimester. Both the second‑trimester and third‑trimester cervical shortening are classified as early or threatened labor, underscoring that cervical shortening at any stage of pregnancy serves as a warning sign for potential premature labor and requires careful monitoring and management to protect maternal and fetal health.
